message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE A CHESS-RESOURCE RESEARCHER. Category: "Endgame Mastery".

Endgames are likely Scrodinger's most undertrained area. Find the best endgame resources for a 1700-2300 player, ordered by difficulty: foundational (Silman Complete Endgame Course), intermediate, and advanced (Dvoretsky's Endgame Manual). Include the best Chessable endgame courses, the best endgame video series (e.g. Saint Louis Chess Club, Naroditsky endgame content), and any interactive endgame trainers (e.g. lichess practice, chess.com endgame drills, Endgame tablebase tools).

METHOD: Use WebSearch to find candidate resources, then WebFetch the most promising official pages (Chessable, publisher sites, YouTube, Amazon, chess.com, lichess) to confirm titles, authors, prices, and URLs. Do MULTIPLE searches with varied queries. Return 6-12 REAL, currently-available resources. Every "why" MUST reference a specific item from Scrodinger's profile (a named weakness, his repertoire, his strength, or his rating goal) — no generic justifications. Be concrete with prices and canonical URLs. Mark each Essential/Recommended/Optional.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, I found information about GM Daniel Naroditsky's endgame video series on YouTube:

Naroditsky has created an endgame video series that includes "Introduction to Pawn Endgames," where Daniel presents one of the most inspiring and beautiful endgames of his career. The series continues with videos on advanced pawn endgame concepts with a detailed overview of proper king use.

The series includes several videos covering different endgame topics:
- Introduction to Pawn Endgames | Principles of Chess Endgames
- Advanced Pawn Endgame Concepts | Beginner to Master | Principles of Chess Endgames
- Knight Vs Bishop Endgame | Principles of Chess Endgames
- Pawn Breakthroughs | Principles of Chess Endgames

These videos are available on his YouTube channel and appear to be part of a broader "Principles of Chess Endgames" educational series.

Links: [{"title":"Finding The Best Endgame Courses On Chessable - Chess.com","url":"https://www.chess.com/article/view/the-best-endgame-courses-on-chessable"},{"title":"Best Endgame Course","url":"https://www.chessable.com/discussion/thread/371460/best-endgame-course/"},{"title":"What Endgame course do your recommend","url":"https://www.chessable.com/discussion/thread/517910/what-endgame-course-do-your-recommend-/517961/"},{"title":"Chessable Roundup: June 21, 2025 - Chessable Blog","url":"https://www.chessable.com/blog/chessable-roundup-june-21-2025/"},{"title":"Best Advanced Endgames Course","url":"https://www.chessable.com/discussion/thread/701949/best-advanced-endgames-course/"},{"title":"Chessable Roundup: March 14, 2025 - Chess.com","url":"https://www.chess.com/blog/Chessable/chessable-roundup-march-14-2025"}]

Based on the search results, here are some of the best Chessable endgame courses for 2025:

**Top Recommended Courses:**

1. **100 Endgames You Must Know** by GM Jesus de la Villa, presented by IM John Bartholomew with Chessable's move trainer. This course teaches important endings likely to come up in your games and develops your ability to convert advantages into wins and to hold bad positions.

2. **Dvoretsky's Endgame Manual**, presented by GM Ewin L'Ami, is based on the definitive endgame guide written by the late IM Mark Dvoretsky. World champion Vladimir Kramnik described it as "An absolute must for every chess professional".

3. **Beginner Mistakes and How to Avoid Them: The Endgame** by GM Josh Friedel teaches you to avoid typical endgame mistakes that haunt players from 1400 to 2000 Elo.

4. **The Fundamentals 2: Boost Your Chess** by Quality Chess & GM Artur Yusupov is a 9-part program that covers opening, tactics, strategy, and endgames.

Chessable is well-known for its endgame content, and these courses are among the top options available on the platform.

From elementary to the most complex endgames, you can practice until you learn how to face all of them. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/supertorpe/chessendgametraining"},{"title":"Chess tactic #FWqqR - White to play","url":"https://lichess.org/training/endgame"},{"title":"[ENDGAMES] Endgame Practice • lichess.org","url":"https://lichess.org/study/iHbj6waL/5I43rQAB"},{"title":"Endgame drills • lichess.org","url":"https://lichess.org/study/uogwMmx4/zekVKUou"},{"title":"Advanced endgame practice • lichess.org","url":"https://lichess.org/study/TxwyRCgu/nFYiUNfF"},{"title":"Better Chess Training: Some Endgame Training Resources","url":"http://www.betterchesstraining.com/2017/03/endgame-training-resources.html"},{"title":"Chess Training - Chess Opening Trainer & Chess Endgame Practice | Free Online","url":"https://chess-analysis.org/train"},{"title":"ENDGAME TRAINER! • lichess.org","url":"https://lichess.org/study/hmD3AXAj/HOVR3sv8"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key findings about endgame practice trainers and resources:

## Dedicated Endgame Training Applications

Chess Endgame Training is a web application that takes advantage of tablebase databases (like Syzygy) and provides an extensive catalog of positions to assist in chess endgame training. The application allows you to play against the Syzygy tablebases or against the Stockfish 16 NNUE engine.

## Lichess Resources

Lichess offers a tactic trainer with endgame positions, and there are various endgame drill studies available on the platform created by the community.

## Chess.com Endgame Drills

Chess.com has added Endgame Fundamentals and Endgame Practice drills with positions specifically chosen as a progression from simple to more complex. Access to the first few drills in each section is free, and access to the rest can be purchased through subscription.

## Other Endgame Training Tools

Chess Analysis offers endgame practice against optimal tablebase play using spaced repetition to help master 50+ essential endgame positions, and this chess endgame practice is completely free with access to all 50+ endgame exercises.

I've completed the web search for your query. Here are the key findings:

## Fundamental Chess Endings by Müller and Lamprecht

This is a masterly one-volume encyclopaedia that covers all major endgames and is the first truly modern one-volume endgame encyclopaedia. It makes full use of endgame tablebases and analytical engines where previous authors could only make educated guesses.

The book was studied intensively by current World Champion Magnus Carlsen in his youth. The authors are Karsten Müller, a German grandmaster, and Frank Lamprecht, an international master from Germany.

## Connection to Capablanca

The search results reveal an interesting connection between classical and modern endgame literature. Capablanca's "Chess Fundamentals" contains example 59, and a similar example appears in "Fundamental Chess Endings" by Müller and Lamprecht as Diagram 6.44 on page 183. Both examples demonstrate the same winning method because the pawn is a bishop's pawn and the Black king is cut off from it by two files.

The modern Müller and Lamprecht work appears to have updated and verified classical endgame principles from earlier works like Capablanca's foundational text using contemporary computer analysis.

content.text: I have all the information I need. Let me compile the curated list of endgame resources tailored to Scrodinger's profile.

resources.name: Silman's Complete Endgame Course: From Beginner to Master
resources.why: This is THE foundational fix for Scrodinger's #5 weakness (undertrained endgame technique). Its unique rating-band structure lets him study exactly the 'Class A / Expert' tier matching his 2010 rapid / ~1600-1700 estimated FIDE and skip what he already knows - efficient for a fast improver who needs to plug a specific gap, not relearn basics.
resources.time: 1 book, ~6-10 weeks reading the tiers up to his level

resources.name: 100 Endgames You Must Know
resources.provider: GM Jesus de la Villa (Chessable / New in Chess)
resources.cost: ~$29.99-$39.99 USD (free book-text tier; paid adds video)
resources.why: Directly targets weakness #4 (losing the thread when converting to a positional/technical game) and #5. Chessable's spaced-repetition move trainer turns the 100 theoretical wins/draws into automatic recall - the same drilling that made his tactics a 2073-2212 strength, now applied to endgames. Knowing these 100 lets him confidently steer his aggressive games into won endings instead of fearing the conversion.
resources.time: 20-40 hrs initial, then ongoing spaced reviews

resources.name: Principles of Chess Endgames (Beginner to Master series)
resources.why: Naroditsky explains the WHY behind pawn breakthroughs, king activity and N-vs-B endings - exactly the middlegame-to-endgame transition judgment (weakness #4) that books alone don't build. Free and verbal, it complements Silman's text and is ideal passive study for a player who already calculates well but needs the strategic narrative of when an endgame is winning.
resources.time: ~10-15 hrs across the series, watch alongside book study

resources.name: Lichess Practice: Endgame studies (Pawn & Rook Endgames)
resources.why: Free interactive drilling of opposition, key squares, 7th-rank rook pawns and basic/practical rook endings against instant feedback. Scrodinger already lives on lichess (puzzles 2073) - this lets him build the technical muscle memory that addresses weakness #3 (calculation not automatic under time pressure) so simple endgames don't bleed his blitz rating.

resources.name: Chess.com Endgame Drills: Fundamentals & Practice
resources.cost: First few drills free; full access via Chess.com membership (~$5-14/mo)
resources.why: Practice-against-the-engine drills with move-quality feedback, on the same platform where his main rating lives (rapid 2010, scrodingxxf). Repeating these converts theoretical knowledge into the automatic technique he lacks when the clock is low - reinforcing the fix for weaknesses #3 and #5.

resources.name: Fundamental Chess Endings
resources.provider: GM Karsten Muller & IM Frank Lamprecht (Gambit)
resources.why: The modern one-volume reference (the one young Carlsen drilled). For a player explicitly aiming CM->FM->IM->GM, this is the comprehensive bridge between Silman and Dvoretsky - organized by material so he can look up exactly the rook or minor-piece ending he just botched. Use as a reference manual to systematically close weakness #5 as his level climbs.

resources.name: Dvoretsky's Endgame Manual (5th Edition, rev. Karsten Muller)
resources.why: The acknowledged advanced standard ('a must for every chess professional' - Kramnik). This is the long-term capstone for Scrodinger's titled-player goal - the blue-text precise-knowledge sections are exactly the deep technique an IM/GM aspirant must master. Not for now, but the destination after Silman + 100 Endgames; tackle once he's a stable 2100+.

resources.name: Endgame Lectures with GM Yasser Seirawan
resources.why: GM-level annotated endgame instruction from real master games, free. Watching how a GM treats the transition into and through the endgame trains weakness #4 (losing the positional thread after the opening) - it shows the strategic plan an aggressive 1.e4 player needs once the attack fizzles and the game must be won technically.

content.text:
I researched and verified endgame resources tailored to Scrodinger's profile (rapid ~2010, tactics 2073-2212 strength, undertrained endgames as weakness #5, conversion/middlegame-transition weakness #4, blitz calculation-under-pressure weakness #3, and the long-term CM->GM goal). Returned 8 real, currently-available resources ordered by difficulty:

Essential
- Silman's Complete Endgame Course (foundational, rating-tiered so he studies exactly his Class A/Expert band)
- 100 Endgames You Must Know on Chessable (spaced-repetition drilling, same method that built his tactics strength)
- Lichess Practice endgame studies (free interactive drilling, platform he already uses)

Recommended
- Naroditsky Principles of Chess Endgames (free, explains the strategic "why")
- Chess.com Endgame Drills (engine practice on his main rating platform)
- Fundamental Chess Endings, Muller/Lamprecht (modern comprehensive reference bridge)

Optional / longer-term
- Dvoretsky's Endgame Manual 5th ed. (advanced capstone for his titled-player goal, once 2100+)
- Saint Louis Chess Club Seirawan endgame lectures (free GM-level instruction)

Each "why" ties to a named profile item (specific weakness number, his Chessable/lichess habits, or his title ambition). Prices and canonical publisher/platform URLs were confirmed via search and page fetches where reachable. Note: the Chessable 100 Endgames page returned only a shell on fetch, so its price (~$29.99-$39.99 with a free text tier) is from search-result data rather than a direct page read.
